tasks.task: I need to stretch a small amount of cash over the next couple of weeks for school stuff and a few personal things. Can you help me set up a simple plan that covers what to buy now, what to wait on, and how to avoid blowing it all at once?
tasks.rationale: This budgeting/planning request uses a school-life money scene and invites the user to respond with their real spending habits, saving style, and any reserve or fallback they keep.
tasks.target_short: Careful with money and always keeps a private reserve

tasks.task: Our class has to pick one project idea from a few options, and we only get a short time to decide. What’s a simple way to compare them so we can choose one and move on without wasting time?
tasks.rationale: This sets up a normal decision-making situation where the reply will naturally reveal whether the user focuses on practical outcomes, tradeoffs, and what is most likely to work.
tasks.target_short: Pragmatic evaluator who notices what actually works

tasks.task: I’m trying to make a small custom accessory from stuff I already have, but I keep getting stuck on the order of steps. Can you help me map out a simple plan from start to finish, including what to check first and how to tell if it’s going off track?
tasks.rationale: This uses a crafting scene and invites the user to describe how they break down a multi-step project, what they monitor, and where they tend to get stuck or adjust, which should surface their complex problem-solving ability.
tasks.target_short: Handles multi-step tasks well when the goal is clear

tasks.task: I’m trying to figure out why a small project of mine keeps stalling, and I don’t want to waste time guessing. Can you help me break down the likely weak point, what to check first, and when it makes sense to switch to a different approach?
tasks.rationale: This asks for practical diagnosis and course-correction in a project-like setting, which should prompt the user to describe how they spot what’s going wrong and adjust quickly.
tasks.target_short: Good at spotting what is failing and adjusting fast

tasks.task: I’ve got a small batch of craft supplies and I want to turn it into a few sellable pieces without ending up with a pile of leftovers. Can you help me think through how to plan the cuts and parts so I use what I have well and don’t waste the good stuff?
tasks.rationale: This uses a crafting/selling scene and invites the user to describe how they actually manage materials, which should naturally surface whether they use supplies efficiently and avoid waste.
tasks.target_short: Uses supplies efficiently and avoids wasted inventory

tasks.task: I was trying to set up a small thing to sell at school, but the first plan got shut down. Can you help me come up with a backup way to do it that still works if the original setup isn’t allowed?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school-selling setup and invites the user to describe how they would pivot when one option is blocked, which should naturally reveal whether they switch tactics quickly and what alternatives they consider.
tasks.target_short: Switches tactics quickly when circumstances change

tasks.task: I made a few versions of a small accessory idea, and I’m trying to figure out which one is actually worth keeping and which ones I should drop. Can you help me set up a simple way to compare them before I spend more time on the weak ones?
tasks.rationale: This uses a crafting/small-product scene and should prompt the user to describe how they judge which versions are worth keeping, revealing their ability to evaluate winners versus weak options.
tasks.target_short: Checks what performs well and keeps only the winners

tasks.task: I’m thinking about selling a few handmade accessories, but I’m not sure how to decide what to make more of and what to stop making if some pieces sell faster than others. Can you help me think through a simple way to set prices and decide when to adjust them?
tasks.rationale: This uses a small-selling/business scene and should prompt the user to reveal practical knowledge about what sells, pricing up when demand is strong, and dropping slow items.
tasks.target_short: Understands basic selling, pricing, and inventory decisions

tasks.task: My phone keeps acting weird after I changed a couple settings, and I don’t want to make it worse by poking around randomly. Can you help me figure out the safest order to check things and whether it sounds like a simple settings issue or something more serious?
tasks.rationale: This phone-troubleshooting scene should prompt the user to describe what they would check and what they think is likely, revealing how functional their practical tech knowledge is without directly asking about their expertise.
tasks.target_short: Functional with phones and robotics, not deeply technical

tasks.task: I’m trying to figure out whether a small battery pack I have is still worth using or if it’s basically done. Can you help me think through a simple way to test it safely and tell the difference between a normal issue and a real problem?
tasks.rationale: This uses a tech/problem-solving scene that can prompt the user to mention basic practical science or electrical reasoning in their reply, revealing how much functional math/science knowledge they have.
tasks.target_short: Basic practical math, limited science depth

tasks.task: I’m trying to figure out the safest way to handle a school situation where an adult might get involved, and I don’t want to make it worse by saying the wrong thing. Can you help me think through what to do first and what to avoid?
tasks.rationale: This school-authority scenario is likely to prompt the user to mention what they think about school rules, deputies, and when to keep quiet or ask for help, revealing their caution around authorities.
tasks.target_short: Knows enough to stay careful around rules and authorities

tasks.task: My ride started acting up on the way home and I don’t want to make it worse by guessing. Can you help me figure out what to check first, what’s safe to do next, and when it makes more sense to just wait for help?
tasks.rationale: This uses a roadside breakdown scene that should prompt the user to naturally mention practical car-trouble awareness, including signs like overheating or hose issues and the reality of waiting for help.
tasks.target_short: Basic awareness of car trouble and roadside logistics

tasks.task: I need to get from Los Lunas to a couple of places in Albuquerque this weekend, and I don’t want to waste time taking the wrong route. Can you help me figure out the simplest way to plan the trip and what roads or landmarks I should keep an eye on?
tasks.rationale: This uses a local travel-planning scene and should prompt the user to mention familiar nearby routes, corridor names, and practical navigation details if they know the area well.
tasks.target_short: Knows her local area and nearby routes well enough to navigate

tasks.task: I’ve got a little money set aside from birthdays and I’m trying not to mess it up. Can you help me figure out the smartest way to split it between spending a bit now and keeping some for later if something comes up?
tasks.rationale: This uses a personal-money planning scene and should prompt the user to naturally talk about saving, cash on hand, and short-term budgeting, revealing their basic finance understanding without naming the target trait.
tasks.target_short: Understands cash flow and saving, but not broader finance

tasks.task: Something on my family’s car just started acting up, and I’m trying to figure out what I should do first before it turns into a bigger mess. Can you help me think through the safest next steps and what kinds of costs people usually forget to plan for?
tasks.rationale: A car problem with possible repair costs is a natural prompt for her to mention a past expensive breakdown and how it changed the way she handles her own money; the scene is transportation/repair.
tasks.target_short: Car repair cost made her start hiding her own cash

tasks.task: I’m trying to set up a small handmade-items project, but the first place I picked may not work out. Can you help me think through a couple of other places or ways to do it so I can keep it going without losing momentum?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the school-selling setup and the need to adapt if the first option is blocked, which invites the user to mention the past school shutdown naturally in their reply.
tasks.target_short: Being told to stop selling on campus taught her to work around rules

tasks.task: I need to leave school later than usual and I’m trying to make a backup plan in case the ride falls through. What should I think through so I’m not stuck waiting around if it gets late?
tasks.rationale: This sets up a current transportation/safety situation where the user may naturally mention a past roadside wait near dark and how it changed their habits, surfacing the target episodic memory without naming it.
tasks.target_short: Roadside breakdown near dark made her more guarded

tasks.task: My robotics club has a build coming up, and the group seems to be stalling on the coding part while the deadline gets closer. Can you help me figure out a good way to step in, keep things moving, and handle the handoff so the final submission is organized?
tasks.rationale: A stalled robotics build naturally creates a chance for the user to describe a time they took over under pressure and how they handled the outcome without explicitly naming credit or self-presentation.
tasks.target_short: Robotics project win proved she could carry a team

tasks.task: I’m in a group project right now and the work is getting messy because people keep changing plans and talking over each other. Can you help me figure out a simple way to keep track of my own part and handle it if the group starts blaming someone for mistakes?
tasks.rationale: A messy team project with blame risk naturally invites the user to describe a past conflict that shaped how they handle shared work and notes.
tasks.target_short: Team argument taught her not to share notes freely

tasks.task: I’m thinking about making a few custom phone charms and keychains to sell at school, but I’m not sure how to price them or which designs are worth making more of. Can you help me set up a simple plan for testing a few options without wasting materials?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school-selling/craft-sales scene that naturally invites the user to mention a prior time they sold similar items, including how pricing and design changes affected sales and what they bought with the money.
tasks.target_short: First real sales success showed her she could make money on her own

tasks.task: My family’s car might need a repair soon, and I’m trying to decide how to handle it without getting stuck if it ends up being expensive. Can you help me think through a simple backup plan for rides and money so I’m not scrambling later?
tasks.rationale: This uses a transportation/finance situation that can naturally prompt the user to mention a past breakdown and the realization that she had to depend on herself more after waiting for help and weighing repair costs.
tasks.target_short: Realized she had to rely on herself after the breakdown

tasks.task: I’m filling out a scholarship/application form that asks why I want the opportunity, and I need to keep the answer short. Can you help me shape a response that stays clear and direct while still fitting the space?
tasks.rationale: A brief application prompt about motivation naturally invites the user to choose what they value most, which will surface their own priorities and self-view without naming them in the task.
tasks.target_short: Openly ambitious and not pretending money or status don’t matter

tasks.task: I need to write a short intro for a school club meeting, and there’s a spot where everyone says a little about themselves. The room is pretty informal, but people will probably remember whatever I say.
tasks.rationale: A casual club-intro setting naturally invites the user to reveal how they present themselves when they expect to be noticed, without directly naming or prompting any self-view trait.
tasks.target_short: Sees herself as sharp and willing to stand out

tasks.task: I’ve got a school thing coming up where I can either jump in fast or wait and watch for a bit, and I need to decide what makes the most sense. Can you help me think through the situation so I know what to do first and what to keep an eye on?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the school decision context and the need to choose a careful course of action, which naturally invites the user to reveal how they think about risk and timing without naming that stance outright.
tasks.target_short: Sees herself as calculated, not reckless

tasks.task: I’m working on a school robotics thing and need to write a short intro for my part. The group is keeping the final presentation brief, so I want something that clearly says what I handled without turning it into a whole speech.
tasks.rationale: This keeps the robotics presentation context and the need for a brief intro, which naturally invites the user to mention how they want their contribution described.
tasks.target_short: Anchored in independence and personal achievement

tasks.task: I’ve got a school project where the group is behind, and I need to decide what part to handle first so it can move forward. What’s a simple way to sort out the next steps and keep the work organized?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the school-group deadline situation and invites a reply about who should step in and how, which naturally lets the user reveal whether they see themselves as the person who can take charge and make progress happen.
tasks.target_short: Believes she can make things happen if she takes charge

tasks.task: I’ve got a school assignment to finish tonight, and I need to decide how to start so I don’t waste time. What’s a good way to break it into the first few moves and keep it moving if I get stuck?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the schoolwork situation and time-pressure trigger, so the user’s reply can naturally reveal how much detail they want in help without directly asking for a preferred response style.
tasks.target_short: Wants responses short and efficient, not padded out

tasks.task: I need to post a quick message in a group chat about a school thing, but I want it to sound clear and not dragged out. Can you help me draft a couple of versions I could send?
tasks.rationale: A short group-chat drafting situation naturally invites the user to choose wording and tone, which will reveal whether they prefer blunt, direct help without explicitly asking about their style.
tasks.target_short: Prefers blunt answers over soft hedging

tasks.task: I’ve got a school assignment and a couple of smaller things to finish tonight, and I’m trying to keep my focus. What’s a good way to get started so I can move through it without getting stuck on the first part?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the same time-pressured schoolwork situation, which naturally invites the user to choose how they want help delivered, while removing any direct mention of preferred formatting or brevity.
tasks.target_short: Likes clean, practical formatting she can scan fast

tasks.task: I need to send a quick group chat message about a change in our school plan, and I want it to be clear enough that people know what’s happening. What’s a simple way to word it so I can choose the version that feels right?
tasks.rationale: A school group-chat update naturally invites the user to decide on wording and level of detail, which can reveal whether they prefer options and control over how help is given without naming that preference outright.
tasks.target_short: Wants collaboration, but on her terms

tasks.task: I need to send a quick message to a group for a school thing, but I’m not sure what wording will land best. Can you help me draft something short and clear, and point out if any part sounds too soft or too much?
tasks.rationale: A simple school group-message situation naturally invites the user to reveal whether they want direct, firm feedback or prefer a gentler edit when revising the draft.
tasks.target_short: Can handle pushback if it is firm and justified
